message:
Refine robot dashboard output structure

- Align DashboardOutput ordering for robot dashboard JSON/brief output and keep fleet/system/summary plus mail, conflicts, file changes, alerts, and beads fields.
- Ensure tmux agent traversal updates summary counters while building session snapshots.
- Restore markdown summary section layout for non-JSON dashboard output.

 {"id":"ntm-cs4z","title":"Phase 1: Core Rotation Command (ntm rotate)","description":"# Phase 1: Core Rotation Command - THE ACTUAL MVP

## What This Is
The `ntm rotate` command that delivers the core value: when you hit a rate limit, this helps you switch accounts with minimal friction.

## Why This is Phase 1 (Not Quota Tracking!)
The user's pain point is NOT "I don't know my quota percentage." It's "when I hit a limit, switching accounts is tedious." Quota display is nice-to-have polish, not the foundation.

## Core Flow: Restart Strategy (Default)
This is often the simplest and best approach:

```
1. User runs: ntm rotate myproject --pane=0
2. NTM terminates the current session gracefully
3. NTM prompts: "Switch browser to: backup1@gmail.com, then press ENTER"
4. User switches browser account (outside tmux)
5. User presses ENTER
6. NTM starts new agent session (auto-uses current browser account!)
```

**Key insight**: New sessions automatically use the browser's currently logged-in account. No /login needed!

## Core Flow: Re-auth Strategy (--preserve-context)
For when you want to keep the current context:

```
1. User runs: ntm rotate myproject --pane=0 --preserve-context
2. NTM sends "/login" to the pane
3. Browser opens (or URL displayed for SSH)
4. User does OAuth in browser
5. NTM detects auth success pattern
6. NTM sends continuation prompt: "continue. Use ultrathink"
```

## Implementation

### Command Structure
```go
// internal/cli/rotate.go
var rotateCmd = &cobra.Command{
    Use:   "rotate [session]",
    Short: "Rotate to a different account when rate limited",
    Long: `Helps switch AI agent accounts when hitting rate limits.
    
By default, uses the restart strategy (quit session, switch browser account, start fresh).
Use --preserve-context to re-authenticate the existing session instead.`,
}

// Flags
var (
    rotatePane           int
    rotatePreserveCtx    bool
    rotateAccount        string  // Target account (optional, suggests from config)
    rotateDryRun         bool
)
```

### Restart Strategy Implementation
```go
func executeRestartRotation(session string, paneIdx int, targetAccount string) error {
    paneID := getPaneID(session, paneIdx)
    provider := detectProvider(paneID)
    
    // 1. Terminate session gracefully
    fmt.Printf("Terminating %s session in pane %d...\n", provider, paneIdx)
    if err := terminateAgentSession(paneID, provider); err != nil {
        return fmt.Errorf("failed to terminate: %w", err)
    }
    
    // 2. Wait for shell prompt
    if err := waitForShellPrompt(paneID, 10*time.Second); err != nil {
        return fmt.Errorf("session didn't exit cleanly: %w", err)
    }
    
    // 3. Prompt user for browser switch
    fmt.Printf("\n")
    fmt.Printf("╔════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗\n")
    fmt.Printf("║  SWITCH BROWSER ACCOUNT                                ║\n")
    fmt.Printf("╠════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣\n")
    fmt.Printf("║  Switch your browser to: %s\n", targetAccount)
    fmt.Printf("║  Then press ENTER to continue...                       ║\n")
    fmt.Printf("╚════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝\n")
    
    // Auto-open browser to Google accounts (optional)
    if cfg.Rotation.AutoOpenBrowser {
        openBrowser("https://accounts.google.com")
    }
    
    // 4. Wait for user confirmation
    bufio.NewReader(os.Stdin).ReadLine()
    
    // 5. Start new agent session
    fmt.Printf("Starting new %s session...\n", provider)
    projectDir := cfg.GetProjectDir(session)
    agentCmd := getAgentCommand(provider)
    
    return tmux.SendKeys(paneID, fmt.Sprintf("cd %q && %s", projectDir, agentCmd), true)
}
```

### Re-auth Strategy Implementation
```go
func executeReauthRotation(session string, paneIdx int) error {
    paneID := getPaneID(session, paneIdx)
    provider := detectProvider(paneID)
    
    // 1. Send login command
    loginCmd := getLoginCommand(provider) // "/login" for Claude, "/auth" for Gemini
    fmt.Printf("Sending %s to pane %d...\n", loginCmd, paneIdx)
    if err := tmux.SendKeys(paneID, loginCmd, true); err != nil {
        return err
    }
    
    // 2. Monitor for auth completion
    fmt.Println("Complete the browser authentication...")
    if err := waitForAuthSuccess(paneID, provider, 5*time.Minute); err != nil {
        return fmt.Errorf("auth failed or timed out: %w", err)
    }
    
    // 3. Send continuation prompt
    fmt.Println("Auth successful! Sending continuation...")
    continuation := cfg.Rotation.ContinuationPrompt
    if continuation == "" {
        continuation = "continue. Use ultrathink"
    }
    return tmux.SendKeys(paneID, continuation, true)
}

func waitForAuthSuccess(paneID, provider string, timeout time.Duration) error {
    ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), timeout)
    defer cancel()
    
    successPatterns := []string{
        `(?i)successfully\s+(logged\s+in|authenticated)`,
        `(?i)welcome\s+back`,
        `(?i)authenticated\s+as`,
    }
    
    ticker := time.NewTicker(2 * time.Second)
    defer ticker.Stop()
    
    for {
        select {
        case <-ctx.Done():
            return ctx.Err()
        case <-ticker.C:
            output, _ := tmux.CapturePaneOutput(paneID, 30)
            for _, pattern := range successPatterns {
                if regexp.MustCompile(pattern).MatchString(output) {
                    return nil
                }
            }
        }
    }
}
```

### Helper: Session Termination
```go
func terminateAgentSession(paneID, provider string) error {
    // Try graceful exit first
    exitCmds := map[string]string{
        "claude": "/exit",
        "codex":  "/exit",
        "gemini": "/exit",
    }
    
    if cmd, ok := exitCmds[provider]; ok {
        tmux.SendKeys(paneID, cmd, true)
        time.Sleep(2 * time.Second)
    }
    
    // Check if exited
    output, _ := tmux.CapturePaneOutput(paneID, 5)
    if isShellPrompt(output) {
        return nil
    }
    
    // Force with Ctrl+C
    tmux.SendInterrupt(paneID)
    time.Sleep(time.Second)
    tmux.SendInterrupt(paneID)
    time.Sleep(time.Second)
    
    return nil
}
```

## Testing
- Test with real Claude session (manual)
- Test dry-run mode
- Test timeout handling
- Test graceful vs. forced termination

## Success Criteria
- [ ] `ntm rotate myproject --pane=0` works for Claude
- [ ] Restart strategy completes in <30 seconds (excluding browser time)
- [ ] Re-auth strategy detects success and sends continuation
- [ ] Clear user prompts at each step
- [ ] Handles SSH/remote sessions (displays URL instead of opening browser)

## What This Does NOT Include (Future Phases)
- Quota percentage display
- Account state persistence
- Multi-pane batch rotation
- Auto-trigger on limit detection
- Codex/Gemini support","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","assignee":"BlackHill","created_at":"2025-12-10T15:13:26.343484-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T16:29:00.25906-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-10T16:29:00.25906-05:00"}
 {"id":"ntm-ctt","title":"Add --json support to spawn, create, add, send commands","description":"Implement JSON output for commands that perform actions.

## What This Task Does

Add --json support to commands that modify state or perform actions:
- ntm spawn: Return session details after creation
- ntm create: Return session info after creation  
- ntm add: Return pane info for newly added agents
- ntm send: Return delivery confirmation for sent prompts

## Why These Commands

These commands are commonly used in scripts and automation:
- CI/CD pipelines spawn sessions and need session IDs
- Scripts add agents and need pane indices
- Automated workflows send prompts and need confirmation

## JSON Schemas

### ntm spawn --json
{
  "session": "myproject",
  "created": true,
  "working_directory": "/path/to/project",
  "panes": [
    {"index": 0, "agent_type": "claude", "title": "myproject__cc_1"},
    {"index": 1, "agent_type": "codex", "title": "myproject__cod_1"}
  ],
  "agent_counts": {"claude": 2, "codex": 1}
}

### ntm send --json  
{
  "session": "myproject",
  "prompt_preview": "First 50 chars of prompt...",
  "targets": [0, 1, 2],
  "delivered": 3,
  "failed": 0
}

## Implementation

1. Create output structs for each command
2. Modify command to build struct before any output
3. Use output formatter based on --json flag
4. Ensure errors also return valid JSON when flag is set

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] ntm spawn --json returns session details
- [ ] ntm create --json returns session info
- [ ] ntm add --json returns new pane info
- [ ] ntm send --json returns delivery confirmation
- [ ] Errors produce valid JSON with error field

 {"id":"ntm-cx7","title":"Add --json support to ntm list command","description":"Implement JSON output for the list command.

## What This Task Does

Modify the ntm list command to support --json flag for structured output.

## JSON Schema

{
  "sessions": [
    {
      "name": "myproject",
      "created_at": "2025-12-10T10:00:00Z",
      "attached": false,
      "pane_count": 4,
      "agents": {
        "claude": 2,
        "codex": 1,
        "gemini": 1
      },
      "working_directory": "/path/to/project"
    }
  ],
  "count": 1
}

## Implementation

1. Import internal/output package
2. Create SessionListOutput struct
3. Modify runList to build struct instead of print directly
4. Use formatter to output based on format flag
5. Ensure all existing text output information is in JSON

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] ntm list --json produces valid JSON
- [ ] JSON includes all session information
- [ ] Agent counts are structured as object
- [ ] Empty list produces {"sessions": [], "count": 0}
- [ ] Text output unchanged (default behavior)

 {"id":"ntm-cyq","title":"Implement token estimation library","description":"# Implement Token Estimation Library

## Purpose

Provide rough token estimates for context usage visualization.

## IMPORTANT: Estimation, Not Measurement

We're ESTIMATING tokens, not measuring actual usage. Be honest about this.

## Approach: Character-Based Heuristic

Simpler and more honest than pretending to have accurate tokenizers:

```go
package tokens

// EstimateTokens provides a rough token count estimate.
// Uses ~3.5 characters per token heuristic for English text.
// This is an ESTIMATE - actual token counts vary by model and content.
func EstimateTokens(text string) int {
    // ~3.5 chars per token for typical English
    return len(text) * 10 / 35
}

// EstimateWithOverhead applies overhead multiplier for hidden context.
// Overhead includes: system prompts, tool definitions, conversation structure.
func EstimateWithOverhead(visibleText string, multiplier float64) int {
    visible := EstimateTokens(visibleText)
    return int(float64(visible) * multiplier)
}
```

## Model Context Limits

Approximate limits (may change, conservative estimates):
```go
var ContextLimits = map[string]int{
    "opus":   200000,
    "sonnet": 200000,
    "haiku":  200000,
    "gpt4":   128000,
    "o1":     128000,
    "pro":    1000000,
    "flash":  1000000,
}

func GetContextLimit(model string) int {
    if limit, ok := ContextLimits[normalizeModel(model)]; ok {
        return limit
    }
    return 128000 // Conservative default
}
```

## Why Not Use tiktoken?

1. tiktoken is for OpenAI tokenizers - Claude uses different tokenization
2. Each model family has different tokenizers
3. Character heuristic is 80% accurate and works for ALL models
4. We're showing estimates anyway - precision is false confidence

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] EstimateTokens function with character heuristic
- [ ] EstimateWithOverhead for total context estimate
- [ ] Context limit lookup by model
- [ ] Clear documentation that these are estimates
- [ ] Unit tests validating reasonable estimates","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"SapphireRiver","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:26:08.230758-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T04:00:32.487543-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-10T04:00:32.487543-05:00"}

 {"id":"ntm-hat","title":"Implement state change ring buffer for delta tracking","description":"Track state changes over time to support delta snapshot queries.

## Rationale
--robot-snapshot --since needs to know what changed. This requires maintaining a history of state changes with timestamps.

## Design
Ring buffer of state change events:
```go
type StateChange struct {
    Timestamp time.Time
    Type      string // agent_output, agent_state, bead_update, mail_received, alert
    Session   string
    Pane      string // if applicable
    Details   map[string]interface{}
}

type StateTracker struct {
    changes []StateChange
    maxAge  time.Duration // prune changes older than this
    maxSize int           // max buffer size
    mu      sync.RWMutex
}
```

## Operations
- Record(change StateChange): Add new change
- Since(ts time.Time) []StateChange: Get changes since timestamp
- Coalesce(): Merge multiple output events into one summary

## Persistence
Consider optional file-based persistence for tracking across ntm invocations. Without persistence, delta tracking only works within single long-running session.

## Memory Bound
- Default max 1000 changes or 5 minutes of history
- Configurable via config file","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-10T02:53:57.473297-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T03:20:36.650095-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-10T03:20:36.650095-05:00","comments":[{"id":12,"issue_id":"ntm-hat","author":"jemanuel","text":"## Ring Buffer Design

### Event Types
```go
const (
    ChangeAgentOutput  = "agent_output"   // New lines in pane
    ChangeAgentState   = "agent_state"    // active→idle, etc.
    ChangeBeadUpdate   = "bead_update"    // Status change
    ChangeMailReceived = "mail_received"  // New message
    ChangeAlertNew     = "alert_new"      // Alert triggered
    ChangeAlertResolved = "alert_resolved" // Alert cleared
)

type StateChange struct {
    ID        uint64
    Timestamp time.Time
    Type      string
    Session   string
    Pane      string
    Data      json.RawMessage  // Type-specific payload
}
```

### Coalescing Rules
Multiple rapid events should be coalesced:
- 10 agent_output events in 1 second → 1 event with lines_added=10
- Multiple bead_update for same bead → keep latest
- Alert new + resolved quickly → can optionally suppress both

### Memory Management
```go
type RingBuffer struct {
    changes  []StateChange
    head     int
    tail     int
    capacity int  // default 1000
    maxAge   time.Duration // default 5 min
}
```

### Query Optimization
Maintain index by timestamp for fast Since() queries:
```go
func (rb *RingBuffer) Since(ts time.Time) []StateChange {
    // Binary search to find starting point
    // Linear scan from there
}
```","created_at":"2025-12-10T07:56:02Z"}]}
 {"id":"ntm-hlek","title":"EPIC: Output Deduplication & Comparison","description":"# Output Deduplication & Comparison

## Background & Motivation

When broadcasting the same prompt to multiple agents, users need to understand:
- Did agents produce similar or different results?
- Which files did each agent modify?
- Are there conflicts (same file, different changes)?

## CRITICAL REFRAME: Focus on FILE Changes, Not Text

Original approach: Compare scrollback text with similarity algorithms.
**Problem**: Scrollback is noisy (tool calls, errors, chatter). Comparing it is not useful.

**Better approach**: Compare WHAT AGENTS PRODUCED (file changes).

## What This Feature Actually Provides

### 1. File Modification Tracking
Track which files each agent modified:
```bash
ntm changes myproject
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ File Changes by Agent                                       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ src/api/users.py                                            │
│   Modified by: cc_1, cc_2 (potential conflict!)             │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ src/api/auth.py                                             │
│   Modified by: cc_1 only                                    │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ tests/test_users.py                                         │
│   Modified by: cod_1, cod_2                                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### 2. Conflict Detection
```bash
ntm conflicts myproject
⚠️ Conflict detected: src/api/users.py
  cc_1: +25 -10 lines (added caching)
  cc_2: +18 -8 lines (added validation)
  
  View diff: ntm diff myproject cc_1 cc_2 src/api/users.py
```

### 3. File-Level Diff
```bash
ntm diff myproject cc_1 cc_2
# Shows git diff between uncommitted changes from each agent
# Uses git stash or worktrees if available
```

### 4. Unique Contributions
```bash
ntm contributions myproject
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Unique Contributions                                        │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ cc_1: src/api/auth.py (only agent to touch this)           │
│ cod_1: tests/integration/* (only agent on tests)           │
│ cod_2: docs/api.md (only agent on docs)                    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Implementation Approach

### File Change Detection
Option A: Parse git status per pane (if each pane has isolated changes)
Option B: Track file mtimes before/after prompts
Option C: Use git stash snapshots per agent

**Recommended**: Option A/B hybrid
- Snapshot file mtimes before broadcast
- After agents settle, compare to detect changes
- Attribute changes to agents based on timing

### Conflict Detection
```go
type FileChange struct {
    Path      string
    Agent     string
    Timestamp time.Time
    LinesAdded int
    LinesRemoved int
}

func DetectConflicts(changes []FileChange) []Conflict {
    byPath := groupByPath(changes)
    var conflicts []Conflict
    for path, agents := range byPath {
        if len(agents) > 1 {
            conflicts = append(conflicts, Conflict{Path: path, Agents: agents})
        }
    }
    return conflicts
}
```

## What We're NOT Doing

- NOT comparing scrollback text similarity (too noisy)
- NOT semantic analysis of code (too complex)
- NOT LLM-based summarization (expensive, unreliable)

Focus on concrete, observable file changes.

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Track file modifications per agent
- [ ] Detect same-file conflicts across agents
- [ ] ntm changes shows file attribution
- [ ] ntm conflicts highlights issues
- [ ] ntm diff shows file-level differences
- [ ] Works with git-based workflows","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:27:32.763201-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T17:34:27.468834-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-10T17:34:27.468834-05:00"}

 {"id":"ntm-hzl","title":"Define persona configuration schema","description":"# Define Persona Configuration Schema

## Purpose

Create the TOML schema for defining agent personas.

## Schema Definition

```toml
# ~/.config/ntm/personas.toml (or in config.toml)

[[personas]]
name = "architect"
description = "Senior software architect focused on system design"
agent_type = "claude"      # cc, cod, gmi
model = "opus"             # Model alias or full name
system_prompt = """
You are a senior software architect...
"""
temperature = 0.7          # Optional, agent-specific
context_files = [          # Optional, files to include in context
    "ARCHITECTURE.md",
    "docs/design/*.md"
]
tags = ["design", "review"]  # For filtering/discovery

[[personas]]
name = "implementer"
description = "Fast implementation engineer"
agent_type = "claude"
model = "sonnet"
system_prompt = """
You are an implementation engineer...
"""
temperature = 0.3
```

## Go Types

```go
type Persona struct {
    Name          string   `toml:"name"`
    Description   string   `toml:"description"`
    AgentType     string   `toml:"agent_type"`
    Model         string   `toml:"model"`
    SystemPrompt  string   `toml:"system_prompt"`
    Temperature   *float64 `toml:"temperature,omitempty"`
    ContextFiles  []string `toml:"context_files,omitempty"`
    Tags          []string `toml:"tags,omitempty"`
}

type PersonasConfig struct {
    Personas []Persona `toml:"personas"`
}
```

## Loading Strategy

1. Load built-in personas (compiled in)
2. Load user personas from ~/.config/ntm/personas.toml
3. Load project personas from .ntm/personas.toml
4. Later definitions override earlier (project > user > builtin)

## Built-in Personas

Include 5 default personas:
- architect
- implementer
- reviewer
- tester
- documenter

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] TOML schema defined and documented
- [ ] Go types with proper validation
- [ ] Multi-source loading with override semantics
- [ ] Built-in personas compiled in

 {"id":"ntm-i0zz","title":"Research agent CLI capabilities for prompt/model injection","description":"# Research Agent CLI Capabilities

## Purpose

Before implementing Personas and Multi-Model, we need to understand what each agent CLI actually supports.

## Questions to Answer

### Claude Code
- [ ] How to specify model? (--model flag?)
- [ ] How to inject system prompt? (--system-prompt, file-based?)
- [ ] Does it support temperature control?
- [ ] What environment variables affect behavior?

### Codex CLI  
- [ ] How to specify model? (-m flag)
- [ ] System prompt support?
- [ ] Temperature or other parameters?
- [ ] Environment variables?

### Gemini CLI
- [ ] How to specify model? (--model flag?)
- [ ] System prompt support? (--system-instruction?)
- [ ] Other parameters?

## Research Method

1. Check CLI --help output
2. Read documentation
3. Test with actual commands
4. Document findings

## Output

Create reference document:
```markdown
# Agent CLI Reference

## Claude Code
- Model: `--model <name>` or CLAUDE_MODEL env var
- System prompt: `--system-prompt <text>` or `.claude/system-prompt.md`
- Temperature: Not supported
- Other: ...

## Codex
...

## Gemini
...
```

## Why This Matters

- Personas need to inject system prompts - only works if CLI supports it
- Multi-Model needs to specify models - need correct flag
- Can't implement features for capabilities that don't exist

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Claude Code capabilities documented
- [ ] Codex capabilities documented
- [ ] Gemini capabilities documented
- [ ] Limitations clearly identified
- [ ] Reference document created","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:41:11.699998-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T04:28:18.770992-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-10T04:28:18.770992-05:00"}

 {"id":"ntm-k7o","title":"Integrate --ssh flag with all session commands","description":"Add --ssh support to spawn, list, attach, send, status, kill.

## What This Task Does

After the SSH wrapper (ntm-5zs) is complete, integrate --ssh flag
with all commands that operate on sessions.

## Commands to Update

- ntm spawn --ssh host: Create session on remote
- ntm list --ssh host: List remote sessions
- ntm attach --ssh host: Attach to remote session
- ntm send --ssh host: Send prompt to remote agents
- ntm status --ssh host: Get remote session status
- ntm kill --ssh host: Kill remote session

## Implementation Pattern

For each command:
1. Add --ssh flag (string, optional)
2. Pass to TmuxClient constructor
3. All tmux operations use client abstraction

func runSpawn(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) error {
    sshHost, _ := cmd.Flags().GetString("ssh")
    client := tmux.NewClient(sshHost)
    
    // All operations use client
    if err := client.CreateSession(session); err != nil {
        return err
    }
    ...
}

## Session Host Persistence

When spawning remotely, store host in tmux environment:
client.SetEnvironment(session, "NTM_REMOTE_HOST", sshHost)

When operating on existing session without --ssh:
1. Get session's NTM_REMOTE_HOST
2. If set, use it
3. If not, assume local

## Error Handling

- Connection timeout: Clear message, suggest checking connectivity
- Auth failure: Suggest ssh-agent or key path
- Remote tmux not found: Suggest installing tmux on remote

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] --ssh works on spawn
- [ ] --ssh works on list  
- [ ] --ssh works on attach
- [ ] --ssh works on send
- [ ] --ssh works on status
- [ ] --ssh works on kill
- [ ] Remote host auto-detected for existing sessions

 {"id":"ntm-nuf","title":"E2E Test: Full Agent Workflow","description":"# E2E Test: Full Agent Workflow

## Scenario
Complete lifecycle of spawning, using, and killing an agent session.

## Steps
1. ntm spawn testproject --cc=2 --cod=1
2. Verify session exists with 4 panes
3. ntm send testproject --cc 'echo hello'
4. Verify 'echo hello' appears in Claude panes
5. ntm send testproject --cod 'echo world'
6. Verify 'echo world' appears in Codex pane
7. ntm interrupt testproject
8. Verify Ctrl+C sent to all panes
9. ntm status testproject --json
10. Verify JSON is valid and accurate
11. ntm kill -f testproject
12. Verify session no longer exists

[2025-12-10T10:30:46Z]   STDOUT: Session 'ntm_e2e_12345' created (4 panes)
[2025-12-10T10:30:46Z]   EXIT: 0
[2025-12-10T10:30:46Z] STEP 2: Verify session
[2025-12-10T10:30:46Z]   VERIFY: tmux has-session -t ntm_e2e_12345
[2025-12-10T10:30:46Z]   RESULT: exists
[2025-12-10T10:30:46Z]   VERIFY: pane count == 4
[2025-12-10T10:30:46Z]   RESULT: PASS
...
[2025-12-10T10:30:50Z] TEARDOWN: Killing test session
[2025-12-10T10:30:50Z] TEST END: PASS (5.2s)

 {"id":"ntm-nxf","title":"Integrate context status into dashboard","description":"# Integrate Context Status into Dashboard

## Purpose

Show context usage in the dashboard view for at-a-glance monitoring.

## Dashboard Enhancement

Add context bar to each pane card:
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Panes (4)                                                   │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ ● cc_1   opus      working    [████████░░] 62%              │
│ ● cc_2   sonnet    idle       [█████████░] 89% ⚠️           │
│ ● cod_1  gpt4      working    [███░░░░░░░] 31%              │
│ ● gmi_1  pro       idle       [░░░░░░░░░░]  5%              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Implementation

Add context field to pane model in dashboard:
```go
type PaneCard struct {
    // Existing fields...
    ContextUsed int
    ContextMax  int
    ContextPct  float64
}
```

Render context bar:
```go
func (p PaneCard) ContextBar(width int) string {
    filled := int(p.ContextPct * float64(width) / 100)
    empty := width - filled
    
    color := theme.Green
    if p.ContextPct > 80 {
        color = theme.Red
    } else if p.ContextPct > 60 {
        color = theme.Yellow
    }
    
    return color.Render(strings.Repeat("█", filled)) + 
           theme.Dim.Render(strings.Repeat("░", empty))
}
```

## Refresh Strategy

- Context data cached for 60s
- Press 'c' to force context refresh
- Background refresh if dashboard open >60s

## Keyboard Shortcut

| Key | Action |
|-----|--------|
| c | Refresh context data |
| C | Open full context view |

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Context bar displayed per pane
- [ ] Color coding (green/yellow/red)
- [ ] Warning icon for >80%
- [ ] Keyboard shortcut for refresh
- [ ] Graceful handling when data unavailable","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"WhiteMountain","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:26:13.013354-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T10:49:07.584816-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-10T10:49:07.584816-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ntm-nxf","depends_on_id":"ntm-jda","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:26:23.526527-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}

 {"id":"ntm-oba","title":"Implement --robot-terse for ultra-compact output","description":"Single-line encoded state for extremely token-constrained scenarios.

## Rationale
Sometimes I need just the essentials in minimum tokens. A full snapshot might be 500+ tokens; terse mode gives me the critical state in <50 tokens.

## Usage
```bash
ntm --robot-terse
ntm --robot-snapshot --terse  # terse snapshot
```

## Output Format
```
S:myproject|A:3/3|R:30|B:27|I:3|M:5|!:2
```

Decoded:
- S: Session name
- A: Agents (active/total)
- R: Ready beads
- B: Blocked beads  
- I: In-progress beads
- M: Unread mail
- !: Alert count

## Multi-Session
```
S:proj1|A:2/2|R:15|B:10|I:1|M:2|!:0;S:proj2|A:3/3|R:20|B:5|I:2|M:0|!:1
```

## Use Cases
1. Polling in tight loops
2. Embedding in prompts with strict token limits
3. Quick health checks
4. Dashboard status bars

## Parsing
Provide parsing utilities in robot package:
```go
func ParseTerse(s string) *TerseState
```","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"WhiteMountain","created_at":"2025-12-10T02:52:39.009743-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T10:25:13.886773-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-10T10:25:13.886773-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ntm-oba","depends_on_id":"ntm-sim","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-10T02:54:39.818091-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}

 {"id":"ntm-y7l","title":"Implement agent tagging in pane titles","description":"Store and parse agent tags in tmux pane titles.

## What This Task Does

Extend the pane title format to include user-defined tags:
- Current: session__cc_1
- New: session__cc_1[frontend,refactor]

## Tag Storage Format

Tags are comma-separated within square brackets at the end of pane title.

Examples:
- myproject__cc_1[frontend]
- myproject__cod_1[backend,api,urgent]
- myproject__gmi_1[] (no tags, explicit empty)

## Implementation

Add to internal/tmux package:

func SetPaneTags(session string, paneIndex int, tags []string) error {
    currentTitle := GetPaneTitle(session, paneIndex)
    baseTitle := stripTags(currentTitle)
    newTitle := baseTitle + "[" + strings.Join(tags, ",") + "]"
    return exec.Command("tmux", "select-pane", "-t", target, "-T", newTitle).Run()
}

func GetPaneTags(session string, paneIndex int) []string {
    title := GetPaneTitle(session, paneIndex)
    // Parse [tag1,tag2] from end of title
    ...
}

## Integration with spawn/add

$ ntm spawn myproject --cc=2 --tags=frontend
# Creates: myproject__cc_1[frontend], myproject__cc_2[frontend]

$ ntm add myproject --cod=1 --tags=backend,api
# Creates: myproject__cod_1[backend,api]

## Tag modification

$ ntm tag myproject:1 --add=urgent
$ ntm tag myproject:1 --remove=frontend
$ ntm tag myproject:1 --set=backend,api

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Tags stored in pane title
- [ ] Tags parsed correctly from title
- [ ] --tags flag works on spawn command
- [ ] --tags flag works on add command
- [ ] ntm tag command for modification
- [ ] Tags survive session detach/reattach

 {"id":"ntm-yxa","title":"Implement session agent registration","description":"# Implement Session Coordinator Registration

## Purpose

Register NTM as a COORDINATOR agent with Agent Mail - not as a replacement for the actual agents, but as the orchestration layer.

## IMPORTANT CLARIFICATION

NTM registers as a **coordinator**, not as the agents themselves:
- Identity: `ntm_<session>` (e.g., `ntm_myproject`)
- Program: `ntm`
- Role: Coordinator/orchestrator
- Purpose: User-initiated messages, session-level file reservations

The actual Claude/Codex/Gemini agents may have their own Agent Mail identities (e.g., `GreenCastle`, `BlueLake`). NTM doesn't impersonate them.

## Registration Flow

```go
func RegisterSessionCoordinator(client *agentmail.Client, sessionName, projectDir string) (*agentmail.Agent, error) {
    // 1. Ensure project exists
    if _, err := client.EnsureProject(projectDir); err != nil {
        return nil, err
    }
    
    // 2. Register coordinator identity
    return client.RegisterAgent(RegisterAgentInput{
        ProjectKey:      projectDir,
        Program:         "ntm",
        Model:           "coordinator",
        Name:            fmt.Sprintf("ntm_%s", sanitize(sessionName)),
        TaskDescription: "NTM session coordinator - user-initiated actions",
    })
}
```

## When Registration Happens

- **On spawn**: Register coordinator after session creation
- **On attach**: Update last_active_ts (agent already exists)
- **Optional**: Can skip if Agent Mail unavailable

## Discovering Other Agents

After registration, NTM can discover other agents in the project:
```go
agents, _ := client.ListAgents(projectDir)
// Returns: GreenCastle (claude-code), BlueLake (claude-code), RedStone (codex-cli), etc.
```

This enables the aggregate inbox view.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Coordinator registered as `ntm_<session>` on spawn
- [ ] Registration is idempotent (attach doesn't create duplicate)
- [ ] Other project agents discoverable via ListAgents
- [ ] Registration failures don't block session creation
- [ ] Clear distinction: NTM is coordinator, not the agents","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"ChartreuseLake","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:18:03.476453-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T03:36:01.613618-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-10T03:36:01.613618-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ntm-yxa","depends_on_id":"ntm-uwa","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:19:55.757544-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}
